revision.text:
[[ar:دايجو جي]][[arz:دايجو جى]][[ca:Daigo-ji]][[cs:Daigodži]][[de:Daigo-ji]][[el:Ντάιγκο-τζι]][[en:Daigo-ji]][[es:Daigo-ji]][[fa:معبد دایگو]][[fi:Daigo-ji]][[fr:Daigo-ji]][[gan:醍醐寺]][[it:Daigo-ji]][[ja:醍醐寺]][[ko:다이고지]][[ms:Daigo-ji]][[my:ဒိုင်းဂေါ့ကျောင်းတော်]][[pl:Daigo-ji]][[pt:Daigo-ji]][[sv:Daigoji]][[th:ไดโงจิ]][[tr:Daigo-ji]][[zh:醍醐寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:DaigojiPagoda3242.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|qid=Q10437874|1=Shingon-shū Daigo-ha}}|prefecture={{ill|Fushimi-ku|qid=Q1197771|1=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.95111111|N|135.81944444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Shōbō|qid=Q11610974|1=Shōbō}}|deity={{ill|Bhaiṣajyaguru Buddha|qid=Q854773|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}|part_of={{ill|En no Gyōja Reiseki Fudasho|qid=Q11070136|1=En no Gyōja Reiseki Fudasho}}; {{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto|qid=Q874027|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}; {{ill|Japan's Top 100 Cherry Blossom Spots|qid=Q10312216|1=Japan's Top 100 Cherry Blossom Spots}}; {{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|qid=Q11638581|1=Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground}}; {{ill|Q11627443|qid=Q11627443|1=49 Sacred sites of Saigoku Yakushi}}; {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qid=Q750721|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|qid=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|qid=Q10437732|1=Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama}}}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Daigo-ji | native_name = 醍醐寺 | image = DaigojiPagoda3242.jpg | image_upright = | alt = | caption = The Five-story Pagoda, a {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|qid=Q3245523|lt=National Treasure of Japan|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}} | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = {{ill|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|en|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1197771|lt=Fushimi-ku|1=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} | coordinates = {{coord|34|57|05|N|135|49|15|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | deity = {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (Bhaiṣajyaguru) | rite = Daigo {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon|1=Shingon Buddhism}} | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = http://www.daigoji.or.jp/ | founded_by = [[Shōbō]] |established=874| year_completed = 1997 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Daigo-ji'''|醍醐寺|lead=yes|qq=}} is a {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhist|1=Shingon Buddhism}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|qid=Q44539|lt=Temple|1=Temple}} in {{ill|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|en|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1197771|lt=Fushimi-ku|1=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Its main devotion (''[[honzon]]'') is {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}. [[:en:Daigo (dairy product)|''Daigo'']], literally "{{ill|Ghee|en|Ghee|qid=Q323225|lt=Ghee|1=Ghee}}", is used figuratively to mean "" and is a metaphor of the most profound part of Buddhist thoughts.{{cite book|last1=Soothill|first1=W.E.|authorlink1=William Edward Soothill|last2=Hodous|first2=Lewis|authorlink2=Lewis Hodous|title=A Dictionary of Chinese Buddhist Terms|url=https://archive.org/details/in.ernet.dli.2015.279298|year=1937|qq=}} == History == Daigo-ji was founded in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 115. In 874, Rigen-daishi (Shōbō) founded the temple. After having fallen ill and abdicated in 930, {{ill|Emperor Daigo|en|Emperor Daigo|qid=Q329753|lt=Emperor Daigo|1=Emperor Daigo}} entered Buddhist priesthood at this temple. As a monk, he took the [[:en:Buddhist name|Buddhist name]] Hō-kongō; and shortly thereafter, died at the age of 46. He was buried in the temple, which is why his posthumous name was Daigo.Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran&pg=PP9 p. 134.] During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the temple was devastated by the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, leaving only the five-storied pagoda as the sole surviving structure. Several subsequent reconstruction efforts took place under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Hideyoshi Toyotomi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, with the last reconstruction being carried out in 1606. These efforts rebuilt much of the current structures, including the ''Kondō'' and the ''Kaisan-dō''. In 1939, a forest fire struck the Kami-Daigo portion of the temple, burning down the [[:en:Buddhist Texts Library|Buddhist Texts Library]] and the ''Juntei-dō'', which was only rebuilt in 1968. In 1997, the ''Hokke Sanmaya-do'', a hall that had been originally constructed in 949 but was burnt down in 1470, was reconstructed and renamed as the ''Shinnyo Sanmaya-do''. == National treasures == Several structures, including the ''kondō'' and the five-story pagoda, are {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}. The temple possesses 18 specifically designated national treasures, including the buildings and other works as well; and the temple holds several dozen important cultural assets. Wall paintings at the temple were the subject of academic research which earned the {{ill|Imperial Prize of the Japan Academy|en|Imperial Prize of the Japan Academy|qid=Q4389487|lt=Imperial Prize of the Japan Academy|1=Imperial Prize of the Japan Academy}} in 1960.Japan Academy, Imperial Prize, [http://www.japan-acad.go.jp/en/activities/jyusho/041to050.html#anker001 "Wall-paintings in Daigo-ji Pagoda"], May 18, 1960; retrieved 2011-08-15 As part of the "{{ill|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|en|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|qid=Q874027|lt=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|1=Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)}}", it is designated as a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}. The five-storey {{ill|Pagoda|en|Pagoda|qid=Q199451|lt=Pagoda|1=Pagoda}} at Daigoji temple was built in 951 and is the oldest building in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. It was one of few buildings to survive the [[:en:Onin War|Onin War]] in the 15th century. == Garden == More than seven centuries after its founding, {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} held a famous {{ill|Hanami|en|Hanami|qid=Q210150|lt=cherry blossom viewing|1=Hanami}} party called ''Daigo no hanami''{{cite book |title=Introduction to Japanese Culture |first=Daniel |last=Sosnoski |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |year=1996 |isbn=0804820562 |page=[https://archive.org/details/introductiontoja00dani/page/12 12] |url=https://archive.org/details/introductiontoja00dani|url-access=registration |quote=daigo no hanami. |qq=}} in 1598 at the [[:en:Sambō-in|Sambō-in]] sub-temple.Main, Alison. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=twT6hYuTeSwC&dq=Samb%C5%8D-in+Kyoto&pg=PA27 ''The Lure of the Japanese Garden,'' p. 27.] The bright colors of maple leaves attract tourists and others in the autumn season. {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|qid=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku|1=Emperor Suzaku}}'s mausoleum, known as ''Daigo no misasagi'', is located near Daigo-ji. '' (''momiji'') at Daigo-ji]] == Layout == Daigo-ji is laid out in three parts: [[:en:Sambō-in|Sambō-in]], Shimo-Daigo (Lower Daigo), and Kami-Daigo (Upper Daigo).[http://www.daigoji.or.jp/garan/index_e.html Guide to Daigoji Complex] These are progressively older, wilder, and further up the mountain. Sambō-in and Shimo-Daigo are at the base of the mountain, easily accessible, and attract the most tourism; Kami-Daigo is on top of the mountain, requires a long, strenuous hike to reach, and is accordingly less visited. The streets around Sambō-in can be freely traveled, but entering Sambō-in proper, the museum, Shimo-Daigo, or Kami-Daigo all require separate admission – the first three have combined ticketing, while Kami-Daigo is separate. Sambō-in is a collection of walled complexes, connected by streets lined with cherry blossoms. It contains the temple proper (including a noted tea garden), a museum, and other complexes, and is very lively during cherry blossom season. Shimo-Daigo is one large enclosure, containing detached halls, including the oldest surviving building in Kyoto, together with open spaces. Kami-Daigo, other than a small cluster of buildings at the base, is located on top of the mountain. The entrance to Kami-Daigo can be reached by passing through Shimo-Daigo, or by a path beside Shimo-Daigo. There is a path with stairs up to the top, which takes about one hour to reach the main complex; halfway up there is a resting point and small shrine. At the entrance to the main complex is Daigo-Sui, a spring of holy water, which was the origin of Daigo-ji, together with other buildings. A further fifteen-minute walk reaches the summit, which contains other halls, notably the {{Nihongo||開山堂|Kaisan-dō|Founder's hall|qq=}}, together with expansive views of the cities below. Beyond the summit, the back of the mountain is almost completely undeveloped, primarily featuring hiking trails. There is, however, the {{Nihongo||奥之院|Oku-no-in|inner sanctum|qq=}}, a grotto with a few statues, reached by a twenty-minute hike along a rough trail. Due to difficulty reaching, this is rarely visited by tourists, though a temple event is held there on the first Sunday in March. Near the Oku-no-in is an outlook, the {{Nihongo||東の覗き|Higashi-no-nozoki|east lookout|qq=}}, which offers another view.[http://hayabusa02.blog114.fc2.com/blog-entry-4.html ～４．上醍醐寺＊東の覗き/醍醐寺回峯道～山ある記！] {{in lang|ja|qq=}} == Events == On August 24, 2008, the Juntei Kannon-dō at the top of the hill on the east of the temple was struck by lightning and burned down. It stood in the Kami Daigo part of the temple. Kami Daigo is Number 11 in the 33 temples of the [[:en:Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage|Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]]. The structure dated from 1968.[http://www3.nhk.or.jp/news/t10013667821000.html# NHKニュース　京都 醍醐寺の観音堂が全焼] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080824130001/http://www3.nhk.or.jp/news/t10013667821000.html |date=2008-08-24 |qq=}} Retrieved August 24, 2008 This resulted in the temporary closure of the upper area (Kami Daigo-ji) for disaster restoration construction, which reopened on July 1, 2009.[http://www.daigoji.or.jp/guide_fee.html 拝観案内] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181004034316/http://daigoji.or.jp/guide_fee.html |date=2018-10-04 |qq=}} {{in lang|ja|qq=}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|qid=Q3245523|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}} * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|qid=Q7883225|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)}} * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|qid=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|qid=Q3245521|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] == Notes == {{Reflist|2|qq=}} == References == * Brown, Delmer and Ichiro Ishida, eds. (1979). [ {{ill|Jien|en|Jien|qid=Q715993|lt=Jien|1=Jien}}, 1221], ''{{ill|Gukanshō|en|Gukanshō|qid=Q1368208|lt=Gukanshō|1=Gukanshō}}; "The Future and the Past: a translation and study of the 'Gukanshō,' an interpretive history of Japan written in 1219" translated from the Japanese and edited by Delmer M. Brown & Ichirō Ishida.'' Berkeley: {{ill|University of California Press|en|University of California Press|qid=Q27816|lt=University of California Press|1=University of California Press}}. {{ISBN|0-520-03460-0|qq=}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. * {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|qid=Q332779|lt=Titsingh|1=Isaac Titsingh}}, Isaac. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/{{ill|Hayashi Gahō|en|Hayashi Gahō|qid=Q3128811|lt=Hayashi Gahō|1=Hayashi Gahō}}, 1652]. ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Society of Great Britain and Ireland]]. * Varley, H. Paul , ed. (1980). [[[Kitabatake Chikafusa]], 1359], ''[[Jinnō Shōtōki]] ("A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: Jinnō Shōtōki of Kitabatake Chikafusa" translated by H. Paul Varley).'' New York: {{ill|Columbia University Press|en|Columbia University Press|qid=Q2080179|lt=Columbia University Press|1=Columbia University Press}}. {{ISBN|0-231-04940-4|qq=}} ==External links== * [http://www.daigoji.or.jp/index_e.html official website] * [https://www.worldhistory.org/Daigoji/ Daigoji - World History Encyclopedia] {{Commons Category|Daigo-ji|qq=}} {{Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|qq=}} {{Buddhism topics|qq=}} {{World Heritage Kyoto|qq=}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan|qq=}} [[wikt:creme de la creme|crème de la crème]] [[image:Daigoji Kyoto01s5s4110.jpg|thumb|250px|left|''Kondō'' (Golden Hall) of Daigo-ji,
a National Treasure of Japan]] [[image:Momiji Daigoji4.JPG|thumb|250px|Autumn color from ''{{ill|Acer palmatum|en|Acer palmatum|qid=Q269224|lt=Acer palmatum|1=Acer palmatum}} [[image:Daigoji Sanboin Kyoto04n4592.jpg|thumb|250px|The entrance of Sambō-in]] [[en:Daigo-ji]] [[ar:دايجو جي]] [[arz:دايجو جى]] [[ca:Daigo-ji]] [[cs:Daigodži]] [[de:Daigo-ji]] [[el:Ντάιγκο-τζι]] [[es:Daigo-ji]] [[fa:معبد دایگو]] [[fi:Daigo-ji]] [[fr:Daigo-ji]] [[gan:醍醐寺]] [[it:Daigo-ji]] [[ja:醍醐寺]] [[ko:다이고지]] [[ms:Daigo-ji]] [[my:ဒိုင်းဂေါ့ကျောင်းတော်]] [[pl:Daigo-ji]] [[pt:Daigo-ji]] [[sv:Daigoji]] [[th:ไดโงจิ]]
